speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 11th, 1996. And this is tape number three of my interview with Eva Lex Slomovits. You mentioned earlier that your father brought in a newspaper when the war-- when the war began. What-- how did you get information about what was going on about-- in the outside world in general? How did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- we had an uncle in the town where my father was teaching, and he worked in a-- in a big mill there. And he was like the head of the mill. And somebody had a radio there. And he used to listen to the radio from different countries, the radio. And he would tell the news to my father. Of course, the newspaper only wrote what they wrote, but it was censored, the newspapers-- not like here. So this is how we would get information when my father used to bring a lot of news from the radio.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war, did you know anything about Germans or Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we heard-- we heard about Germany that there was a big-- a lot of anti-Semitism. But we never heard of camps or things like that. But I remember one girl came from Poland, and she told us horror stories. And we thought she was crazy. We couldn't believe it that really, that's what they're doing to people. It was an unbelievable thing. So she was going from town to town and telling these stories, and we thought she was crazy. But later on we found out she was from Poland. She-- she says they have-- there-- they take people and they kill them and then the Germans occupy the country and the-- the Germans take the people and kill them. And I says, that's impossible because my father used to go to Germany. He used to tell us what nice people they are. He used to deal with them. And he used to come home and tell us all the stories about how nice they lived. All week long, let's say, he would go to Berlin. He would visit different temples. And he told us about the-- a reformed temple. And we'd never heard of a reformed temple. He says that the women go to-- to shul and they would pray, say Kaddish, for their parents. And that was so far removed from us. A girl would never go near a [INAUDIBLE] or pray. And he would tell us different. So we knew a lot of things from my father because he traveled a lot. And the-- the Germans, we didn't-- we didn't have any connection with the Germans until they occupied the country. But the Hungarians, they organized the people that lived in the town. Like I said, they organized that was called-- not Hitler Jungen-- it was like Hitler Jugend-- they called them Levente. And this Levente, boys they were are so hostile to Jewish. They would take the Jewish boys from-- from [INAUDIBLE]. They's pull out those kids-- poor kids. They had to shovel their snow, and they had to clean their boots, and they-- and they used to beat up the Jewish boys. And we were really afraid, like we weren't allowed to go on the train. But sometimes if we snuck, we-- were were watching the Levente shun squeal. They were the one that they squeal to the authority on us, the Levente. And these were boys that went to school with my sisters. They were older. They were, let's say, 18, 19 years old. And they belonged to this party. They called themselves Levente. And they were-- we were so scared of them. Saturday we would go to shul. He's-- one said-- and they would say we have to shake their hand and kiss their hand. And my father says, I'm going to shul. I'm not going to do that. He said, you come tonight and I'll explain you why. And he came. He wasn't afraid of my father. And he came. And he says-- my father says to him, when I go to shul, you don't disturb me. He says, because I'm going to pray to God. He says, if you have anything, you come after the shul.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember how he-- he was angry at my father. And we were so scared that he'll do something bad-- that he'll report him or do something bad. But my father was brave enough to stand up against him. He says, I don't want you should ever interrupt me. When I go to shul, you don't interrupt me. I don't interrupt you when you go to shul.

speaker: interviewee
text: And something I remember very clear when this particular guy, he was more than happy to do this. And he came one Saturday that we have to go-- the girls have to go to church. And I never walked into a church. The-- this was-- and we started to cry. And I says, please, we don't want to go to church. And my father says to him, you walk out from the house and the kids will go with you to church. Because my father knew better than if you're not going to go, then he will report us to the higher authority. So we start to cry and screaming. And when he walked out, my father sat down with us. And he says, look, you go to church. You are raised Jewish and you don't believe in Jesus Christ. But a time-- this is such a bad times for us that we have to survive, we have to live according the way they want us to live. But we know in our heart that we are Jewish and we have to observe Judaism how much we can. And don't be afraid to go to church. And I was happy. My father gave me permission to go to church. We didn't have to kneel down, but we had to go to church on Saturday afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did this start? When did this start, that you had to go to church?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- this-- I think in 1943, 194-- like in 1943. And I just was so upset about going to church. And a lot of the girls were laughing. Oh, you're coming with us to church. Are you going to cross yourself? But they didn't make us to pray. And they didn't make us to kneel down. But we sat in church crying. The first time I cried, the second time I cried, and then I stopped crying. I just went. But this Levente, they were-- they were Hitler Jugend in Hungar-- by the Hungarian. And they were after the Jewish girls, and they could throw snow at us. And we were really scared of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Levente means what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't know. That's what they called themselves. I think it's-- it's like Hitler Jugend. They were the young boys that joined the-- the party from the Hungarians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when did your education stop?

speaker: interviewee
text: My education stopped I think when I was 12, 13 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And all-- all the children had to stop going to school then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- at what-- At that point, how did you occupy your time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had what to do. It wasn't that I didn't have what to do around the house and the field. I had what to do. But the thing is that I was missing out on my education. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was nobody to teach us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned earlier that your father used to travel to Germany, and that he got along well with the Germans. This was true even after the Nazis came to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But once the Nazis came in it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Then my mother died in 1936 already. So he didn't go yet before. When my mother got sick. He didn't go maybe in 19-- he never told us about Nazis. We never heard of Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he didn't travel anymore after your mother died?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how was his work affected by the occu-- by the occupation of the Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't affected because it was only affected so much because we were afraid the Hungarian-- the road that my father would take-- he used to drive a bicycle to go to work. And the road that he was taking, a lot of trucks went there to the Carpathian Mountains. They were up-- going to Poland to-- to the front.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Hungarians, the Germans, used that road. And they-- if they saw a Jew with a beard, they would throw things at them. So my father couldn't go with a bicycle anymore with that road. There was no other road, so he used to go through the fields and walk. So we were already, all the children, and we'd beg my father not to come every night home to-- because it was too difficult, in the winter time, especially. And he-- he would say, I work very hard and I want to come home because [SOBBING] when I come home, this is my pleasure to see my family. So we were scared he should come home. So many times he would sleep over because we had an aunt there living in that town and he would sleep over there. But we-- if the weather wasn't good, so he wouldn't come because he couldn't come with  a bicycle, he had to come through the fields. So that's how much it affected, but he still was teaching there. He still got his salary. He-- it was-- didn't affect his work that way, except that-- the transportation, the walking, and he couldn't go on a train. So that's how it affected him. Was religious community life affected during that period of time? Were there any laws?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was affected. Our shul also had not a Jewish person that took care on our shul and made fire in the winter time, because we didn't have-- we had wood-- wooden stove-- stoves that used wood to make fire. So a Jew wasn't allowed to-- a Christian wasn't allowed to work for Jewish people. So the person that took care on the shul, he wasn't allowed to work either.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the shul was cold. And on Saturday we would go to shul-- the girls didn't go at all. And the men would go, and they would make it very fast because it was very cold. In the Carpathian Mountains, it was very-- we had very strong winters. So it affected, because the people couldn't get together so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And like I said, this Levente harassed the Jewish people with the beards. And they harassed the boys from the-- from the Kheyder. And once I remember the Kheyder didn't have a place in our town. Everybody-- let's say, if we had two boys, so we had two weeks Kheyder in our house. We gave them a room and that room. So I remember the Kheyder was in our house and the Levente came with one gen-- two gendarme came. And one of my uncles was the rabbi. And they took my uncle and my-- the kids. They gave them a book. And they took them all around the town. And they were taking him to the city hall. And we knew when they going to get to the city hall with my uncle, that they're going to hit him. So my aunt ran fast to somebody that knew the president-- the, like-- he was like the president from our shul. He was close with somebody from the gendarme. They shouldn't hit my uncle. But they pulled his beard, the gendarme. Walking to the city hall, they pulled his beard. And they were hitting the kids with the rifle. And this was what happened. We were afraid. So it aff-- it affected the Jewish community because we were afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there actual restrictions on-- on-- on their practicing religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't know if there was. I don't-- I don't remember. I don't remember they should restrict, except, like I said, we were afraid. And-- and Sunday we would go to the fields. There once-- once a gendermarie-- my sister was working as a seamstress. So he came in. So he says, do you have permission to-- to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we didn't-- a seamstress didn't never had any permission to work. So he took away the sewing machine from us. And he took it away, and we went to the different town, me and my sister. We knew somebody that lived there near the-- near the gendarme, and they got us the sewing machine out. But they-- they harassed us. They-- they always came up, what can they do? Which sister was this that was a seamstress?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ilona. She was two years older than I was. She was the seamstress.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned earlier that there was a man who was deported to Poland who came back. You also mentioned there was a woman who came from Poland and they started hearing things. Were there-- did you have a lot of refugees coming through your part of the-- your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just-- I just know of this woman. And I remember she asked us if we should wash and comb her hair because she had lice. And it was such a terrible thing. We made her-- got her a bath. We warmed up some water, she should bathe. And we gave her some clothes because she says she didn't take a bath for four weeks or something. And she-- she came, she says she ran away from Poland. But actually, we kids thought that something was the matter with her. But now I know, nothing was the matter. She was just so scared. And she didn't want to stay. She didn't want to sleep because I guess she didn't trust people. So she was running from place to place. But my cousin that ran away-- he was in hiding, and it was a big secret that he's-- he came back. And I saw him once, he came, this-- this cousin, my mother's cousin. And he was, like, afraid even to talk. But he told my aunt what happened and my aunt told us what happened to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get any more information about what was going on with Jews in Poland or other parts of Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I-- I didn't-- I didn't know. I tell you, I was young. And it-- it affected me more close, what was happening in the around, not far away. Until-- it really didn't hit me, it didn't affect me so.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things really begin to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the Hungarians came in it began to change. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned the harassment going on through 1943. At what point do things really begin to get worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I think with the-- it was always-- it was getting like worse and worse, you know? Things weren't getting better. Things were getting worse. And we were-- whenever we complained to my father about it, that it's so bad-- and it wasn't only this part that it was bad. It was also that we were already hungry. We were already-- we got-- we had rations for flour, for sugar. They rationed everybody. So a Jew couldn't go in the line until everybody didn't get their there. And by the time we went to get our flour or sugar, there was no more left.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had an aunt that had a bakery in another town-- that I told you, my cousin, we went to college-- this aunt, they had a bakery. And they gave out-- took in another Jewish person to run the bakery. So I went to that next town to get the bread. I gave them the tickets and she would give me the bread and I brought it home. So we were already hungry. We were harassed. We didn't go to school anymore. So things were very, very tough on-- on the Jewish people. And if we told my father, he couldn't help us. But he always tried to comfort us, that it's like a hurricane, a storm, that it's going to pass by. And as long we're together, we're going to make it. And we've got to help one another. And what could we help one another if there was no food? There was no-- they took away the-- I mean, the fields-- we sold fields. But until we had the business and we-- the person would bring us wheat and potatoes if they didn't have money. And they wanted petroleum or sugar. So they would give us beans and potatoes. And then we sold it and we had money.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they took away our license, so we didn't have the grocery. We only had what my father earned, which wasn't enough. And even if we had the tickets for-- for bread, we couldn't buy it. It was-- my aunt also risked to give us because we weren't from the same town and we were Jewish. But she did it for us [INAUDIBLE]. She-- in hiding, she would bring us-- like, she would carry it out from the house and she would leave it in the field, and I would pick it up there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What town did your aunt live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] . That's where my father was born. And he had there, I think, three sisters living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain in Zarici?

speaker: interviewee
text: How long?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you-- did you remain there, until when?

speaker: interviewee
text: We remained there until 1944, just Pesach. A day-- a day after Pesach they took us away. It happened like this that we-- the last day of Pesach we went for a walk. And the boys and girls, you know, we went for a walk near the river and then we went up on the road.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the road we saw a Jewish man with a beard. And he was riding a horse. And we says, what is he doing? What-- what-- how come a Jewish man is riding a horse on a holiday? So he says, what are you-- where are you going, kids? So I says, no place. What are you doing on the horse? So he says, what I'm doing on the horse, I'm going to tell people that they're taking away all the Jews. I says, where are they taking them? So he says, they're taking them into a ghetto. I says, what is that? I never heard that word, ghetto. That was the first time. So he says, you better go home and you tell your parents that they should prepare themselves that they're going to take aw-- they're taking away all the Jews. They already took away the Jews from my town, so I ran away with a horse. So we came home and we told my father and my aunt-- my mother's sister, she-- the one that lived with us-- that they're taking away-- a man told us they're taking away the Jews in the ghetto. Says, what is a ghetto? We didn't know what a ghetto is. So here we were, like sitting and waiting all night. We didn't go to sleep. We were sitting they should come and take us. And in the morning-- oh, they sent-- in the evening, they sent somebody, like he was a watchman from the city hall. They sent-- we were under house arrest.

speaker: interviewee
text: They sent this man and he says, look, I have to watch the house, that nobody should leave the house. So he was there sitting and he was a nice man. We knew the man because, from the town. But he-- he says, you can't go out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had an outhouse. So if we had to go to the bathroom, he would accompany us and stay there-- there and wait for us. So in one way we said, you know, we have service. We're not afraid to go out at night. So this man watched.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the morning the principal from our school came with two gendarm-- gendarme. And a horse and wagon came also. And the-- this hor-- this wagon was from the peasants. They asked them to take us away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the principal walked in, and my father was very close with the principal. He was a friend. They-- they used to sit and talk all the time and he says-- and he always said to my father in Hung-- they spoke Hungarian, by the way. He says, if you-- if it wouldn't be the way it is, your children would have full scholarships to go to college. He says, maybe someday, he says, I'll give them scholarship to go to college.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he liked us. We felt, you know, that he liked us. So he says to my father, look, Lax, they're taking away all the Jews. You only could take 20 kilos. But he says, don't worry. You could take more. Nobody's going to weight it. He says, take as much as you can because you-- you-- you will need it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father says to him, do you  know where we're going? So he says, I really don't know where they're taking you. But he says, but I know that we ordered horse and wagons to go to Irsava. This how-- how-- what I know, that you're going to Irsava. From there we don't know where they're going to take you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, but take along clothes because there it was still cold, you know, in the [INAUDIBLE]. So my father took-- we got so, so hysterical when it came. We-- we waited for them, and then when it came, we were so upset. We started crying. And my father took us together. He says, don't cry. Things will be OK. Things will be OK. We went up on-- on the horse and wagon. I didn't even want to take anything. I-- I just went up, crying. [SOBBING] And this guy that had the horse and wagon, he-- he felt for us. He-- he went with my sister, Lilly, to school. And he says to her, don't worry, I'll pick you up. I'll take you there, and when the time will come, I'll pick you up again. He promised to pick us up. And from there we went on the wagon and they took us to the city hall. And we came, all the Jewish wag-- the horse and buggies with the wagons, we came to the-- near the city hall. And there were so many persons in the Levente. And they were screaming, we're getting rid of the Jews. And they said, go to Palestine. They told us we should go to Palestine. And I didn't-- I covered my head. I didn't even want to look at them. [SOBBING] It was so terrible. So I said, when they took away my-- my cous-- from-- my mother's cousins, to Poland, the next day they came and they opened up the doors, and they took out the furniture and the clothes and everything. And we were just standing and watching. [SOBBING] And they said, now we could go already to their homes and take it. And I didn't even want to hear. I didn't want to look at nobody. [SOBBING] We were just so depressed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was already glad that we 're leaving the town. So we left the town and we came to-- to this Irsava. First we-- we came to this-- it was a big synagogue there in Irsava. So they put us down there. And from there they said-- like they put, in one house, they took a few streets they-- and they said that this is the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't figure out what a ghetto looks like. Then I found out that a few streets, they made it into a ghetto. So they put us-- we were-- my mother had two sisters, like I said. The two sisters had-- one had five children, one had six children, and we were five. And I forgot all about to tell you how my-- my sister already came to America after my mother died. I go back to this. When my mother died, my father says, there's nothing for us here anymore and we're going to immigrate to America. And he had family here and he-- he wrote my mother's cousin. He wrote to my mother's cousin that my mother died, and he's left with six children. And he says he would like to come to America if she could send us papers to America. So she first sent an affidavit for my sister, my older sister. She was at that time 15 years old. And in no time then, she got a number and she-- the quota. And she was a minor, so my father took her to Prague. And the-- and from Prague, she-- she came to America. And she was always very homesick. She used to write letters and-- And then my father tried-- got the papers to go to America and we were waiting for the quota, you know. And he wrote-- I have the letter, because my sister kept the letter-- that he had-- she had the letter that my father begged her to go to Washington. He should get the number, the quota, because he had-- he had a ticket for a boat. He had all the papers. All he had to have is a number. They should make an exception because things are getting bad there. Well of course, they didn't make an exception. So my sister came here. And he-- we-- we wound up and my father didn't need a quota by the Germans. He went into there he-- he was able to go. So-- so we came to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we continue, we're going to stop here to change tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
